text_clean: Yeah everything in an Australian city is a suburb. Inner-city Melbourne counts as a "suburb" of Melbourne. videoTitle: Should you live in Melbourne? | Pros and cons of living in Melbourne
text_clean: Jamie I'm also from Perth (north of the river, not too far from the ocean) and have also lived in Melbourne for years and years. I miss Perth/WA beaches too! 😭 I was a "water baby" growing up, thankfully my lifestyle isn't beach-dependent as an adult but I'd still like to have the option of being able to go to a great suburban beach without leaving the city. I actually like the changeability of Melbourne's weather, for the most part, because I know that if there's a scorching 40 degree day there's a good chance of the heat breaking tomorrow. A reprieve. Whereas in Perth summers it's just way too damn hot, over and over. And I empathise with those memories of musicians skipping Perth! I'll add a pro: Because there are lots of people in Melbourne who are from elsewhere, I think it's easier to strike new friendships, people on the whole are less insular and less limited to their existing/longstanding friend groups. And a con: How long the winters are. I don't mind the cold so much, but winter seems to start earlier and end much later here than in Perth.

I guess it also depends on people's preferences, i.e., tanning, picnicking, amenities, and the like. videoTitle: Does Sydney have better public transport than Melbourne? (ft. Philip Mallis)
text_clean: Point 7 cannot be stressed enough - it is key: speed and frequency of express-running medium-distance trains but also what it allows you to do. Sydney developed quad (4 tracks) all way out into middle and outer suburbs early on (up to St Marys on T1; up to Hurstville on T4; up to Revesby on T8; up to Rhodes plus another section from West Ryde to Epping on T9; and up to Belmore for separated freight on T3), meaning express running on all main lines for outer-suburban, interurban and intercity trains was simple and easy. Whereas Melbourne only developed triple (3 tracks) into the middle suburbs - and even then only on 3 main lines (Frankstone line to Moorabin along with quad track from South Yarra to Caulfield; Lilydale/Belgrave line to Box Hill; Werribee line from Newport to Werribee). Track speeds within Metro Melbourne are limited to 80kmh where Sydney has many lines with decent track sections of up to 100-115kmh. What this all means is Sydney has regular and (relatively) fast express trains allowing you to get from places 20-30km away from the city like Sutherland, Blacktown, Revesby or Hornsby into the city consistently quicker than equivalent areas in Melbourne, with more flexibility to handle disruptions - and many of these journeys will be shortened further when Sydney Metro opens. What this also allows Sydney to do is split inner and outer sections of lines to run different services: on top of the conversion of the whole T3 to Metro, the inner sections of the T2 T4 T8 and T9 may eventually see a similar conversion to rapid transit with high-capacity high-frequency operations at all inner stops, with suburban inter-urban and intercity services focused purely on the middle and outer suburbs. Melbourne cannot do this without building new lines. Melbourne's trams really aren't that special in performance terms; of the 7 busiest routes in Melbourne (3, 11, 59, 75, 86, 96 and 109) only Route 96, a former heavy rail line, comes close to the L2 & L3 in Sydney in terms of patronage/km or patronage/stop, and all Melbourne lines have slow average speeds (15-18kmh) even outside the city area with many shared traffic sections causing trams to get stuck in traffic, while not many stops have level boarding & disability access. Where Melbourne does have an advantage is coverage, there are no major areas of the city as poorly covered by heavy or light rail in Melbourne as in Sydney (Northern Beaches, SE suburbs, outer SW around Bonnyrigg and Wetherill Park) except perhaps Doncaster (which is planned to be linked with Suburban Rail Loop). Another advantage for Melbourne is being able to run its 3 main regional services (Ballarat, Bendigo and Geelong lines) on the one Regional Rail Link direct and at quite high speeds, whereas all Sydney's regional trains split up quite close to the city and have comparatively slow speeds, hence why NSW was doing planning on an entirely new High Speed Rail network to Newcastle, Wollongong, Canberra/Southern Highlands and Bathurst/Orange - which again would be difficult to implement without expensive city access lines to meet at Parramatta or Olympic Park.

videoTitle: Does Sydney have better public transport than Melbourne? (ft. Philip Mallis)
text_clean: Quad track will be completed on the T1 Northern Line in the next few years between Strathfield and Epping and triplication between Epping and Hornsby as the next stage of the Northern Sydney Freight Corridor program, which is jointly funded by the State and Federal governments. In the longer term, it is proposed to complete the full quad between Strathfield and Hornsby. This will allow greater flexibility for all stations suburban services to be separated from semi-express suburban, express intercity, long distance regional and freight services. Similarly, other long term proposals are to extend the existing quad track on the T1 Western Line from St Marys to Penrith and on the T4 Illawarra Line from Hurstville to Sutherland. Other major infrastructure upgrades which are worthy of consideration are extending the quad on the T8 East Hills Line from Revesby to Glenfield; quadding the T8 South Line from Glenfield to Macarthur, which includes the existing Southern Sydney Freight Line; and notwithstanding Metro West, sextuplication of the T1 Western Line between Homebush and Granville to allow more suburban and intercity express services from the outer west to run through to Sydney Terminal (Central) on the underutilised Main tracks without the need to interchange to Metro West. The digital signalling and ATO upgrades, which don't get much publicity, will allow for higher frequencies up to 24 trains/hr per line and faster journey times. I think it's unlikely that there will be any further metro conversions of existing suburban lines, although compatible single deck suburban trains could potentially operate at higher frequencies on the inner suburban lines at far less cost and disruption to services compared with a conversion. This was proposed prior to the separate metro network coming onto the scene. Proposed future inner suburban cross regional links, fully segregated from the existing suburban network, would be more appropriate as metro lines, including a potential rail link to the Northern Beaches. The Minns' government cancelled the Regional HSR project as it is too expensive and beyond the State government's resources. The best we could hope for is incremental upgrades to the existing lines with curve easing and limited realignments to allow for higher average speeds and a reduction in journey times. This could be helped with Federal funding once they realise that a HSR line from Brisbane to Melbourne isn't viable in the Australian context and instead shift their focus to upgrading the existing regional and interstate network to Medium Speed Rail standard (200km/h).

videoTitle: The Comprehensive "Metro" Network of Melbourne
text_clean: @jdillon8360 The Burnley and Northern loops still reverse direction. The reason for the reverse of direction is largely historic, but there were two reasons: 1) Most of the trains arriving at Flinders Street in the morning were from the east, then had to reverse into the face of following arrivals to stable in the Jolimont yards. And the reverse in the afternoon. 2) One of the problems was the capacity of Flinders Street to handle the number of passengers. If trains arrived at Flinders Street in the morning, then around the loop, or came around the loop to Flinders Street in the afternoon to then run to the ends of the lines, they expected that most passengers would still use Flinders Street, so the problem would not be solved. So it was basically to force passengers to consider using a different station. In the afternoon, the only way to get from Parliament to Richmond, without going right around the loop, is to catch a Burnley Loop train. If the Burnley Loop was altered to run anti-clockwise all day, a lot of passengers would be disadvantaged. If it was altered to run clockwise all day, there would be no way to get directly from Flagstaff to Southern Cross in the morning. Things like probably will change eventually, but there are complexities to those decisions that most don't appreciate.

videoTitle: The Comprehensive "Metro" Network of Melbourne
text_clean: @jdillon8360 the reason is more historical than anything. Its just always been that way since the beginning. The reason the City Circle does not reverse anymore was because going anti-clockwise around that tunnel forces everything across a single flat junction at Jolimont in both directions which created a lot of conflict and severely limited capacity on the Clifton Hill group. In terms of the Caulfield loop, there are two reason why this loop now always runs anticlockwise. The first is because the Selective Door Opening Beacons have only been installed in one direction. An HCMT running the reverse direction cannot release its doors at any loop station. The second reason (and in my own opinion the most useful) is that it allows a direct connection from the Loop into Southern Cross and Flinders Street stations. Until the Caulfield Loop was fixed in one direction, you could not get to those two stations from the loop in the PM Peak without first leaving the loop to North Melbourne, Richmond or Jolimont and then changing trains. This was especially a problem for anyone trying to connect to a regional train at Southern Cross, especially now that VLines ridership is quite high now. This also put enormous pressure on the Trams heading towards Spencer St for the same reason.

videoTitle: The Comprehensive "Metro" Network of Melbourne
text_clean: The weakness of Melbourne's system that doesn't come out in this video is how spread out it is, which means that at the outer extremities where the population growth is, you are rarely very close to a station. As these locations are beyond the spread of the tram network and bus services are poorly planned and infrequent, they become heavily car-dependent. And Mellbourne is still very city-centred compared to Toronto and Sydney, hence the radial network. Infrastructure like the Suburban Rail Loop (which is far from an outer-suburb service) will need a big improvement to bus services to be useful because its stations will be few and far between. We are going to need improvements such as extending the Cranbourne line to Clyde, electrifying the lines to Melton and Tarneit, and the Geelong fast rail to free up capacity on the western suburbs. Improvement to existing services in strategic areas could give better bang for buck than the high-profile vote-winning expansion projects.

It can be very straight forward and easily understood when you're standing there with the signs and orientated to the landmarks and only having to worry about the parts that are relevant to your trip while still being a confusing mess when taken in it's totality with only maps and reports to go from. Also? Plenty of millenials (now in their thirties, mind you) have common sense. Plenty of Gen-X and boomers are ignorante and oblivious idiots. The reverse is also true. It's not a generational thing at all. Not only is common sense not so common, but an awful lot of people can't tell it from "thing I've had time to get used to, and thus have already learned, while the other person hasn't."

videoTitle: Why Melbourne Feels Different Than Sydney
text_clean: Hi Lydia, We are so glad to hear that the femicide history was interesting. We didn't want to be Debbie Downers, but we thought it was a thought provoking bit of street art that we appreciated learning about. We currently are in Nelson, New Zealand. It's a bit slower paced, so we're using it as a place to catch our breath before we head to Queenstown in the New Year. We wish you a very happy Christmas and New Year's as well! —Judy

videoTitle: Why Melbourne Feels Different Than Sydney
text_clean: Hi Peter, It would have been more expensive had we rented a car or done more touring. But we knew we'd have some faster travel in New Zealand because there was a lot we wanted to see, and adding in Hobart where we did a lot of activities, we felt Melbourne could stand on its own (especially with such unpredictable weather we had). We had such a great time. Thanks for watching and have a great Christmas and New Year's! —Judy

videoTitle: Why Melbourne Feels Different Than Sydney
text_clean: Hi @Alistarc1969 Thank you so much for watching! While the rain impeded some things we wanted to do, we still had a great time! And while it's possible to find good coffee in the US, you really have to look for it, and not all the baristas are as consistently good as we had in Melbourne. We loved our time in Tassie and we just released a video from there! SE Asia is in another week or so after we finish with New Zealand! —Judy

videoTitle: Why Melbourne Feels Different Than Sydney
text_clean: Hi Peter, We felt like Phillip Island and the penguin parade probably didn't need us talking about it and potentially bringing even more tourists to a place where penguins just want to do their own thing. And the rain did play a small part in that decision as well. Thanks for watching! —Judy

videoTitle: Why Melbourne Feels Different Than Sydney
text_clean: Hi @nickb5311 We absolutely will want to come back and explore more. We originally had planned to stay longer, but we were worried about how much it would blow our budget, especially since we also wanted to get to New Zealand. Now that we've been there, we'll come back for better weather to explore the SEQ region. —Judy

videoTitle: Why Melbourne Feels Different Than Sydney
text_clean: Hi @nothancz We had hoped that Australia would be significantly less cold than Europe, and while it was (mostly), you're right that there was still a lot of rain. We maybe could have worked backwards and started in Asia and then ended in Australia and New Zealand for better weather. Thanks so much for watching! —Judy

videoTitle: Why Melbourne Feels Different Than Sydney
text_clean: @TheEW1850 Yes, I remember it in the 90s. I worked in hospitality at the time. I actually worked at Dogs Bar in St Kilda where we took coffee very seriously and didn't even offer skinny milk. One year I won the Acland Coffee Challenge when I competed against baristas from other cafe's in Acland st. Ah the good old days.
